utterances: Well, U.S.
District Judge Jesse Furman wrote a very thorough opinion.
It's really, you know, more than 270 pages written by someone who's expecting a higher court to look over his shoulder.
You know, I was there every day for the trial in New York over the citizenship question.
And Furman has said that he's expecting his decision to be appealed, so he put out a very carefully worded opinion.

And some of the main points here is that - one of them is essentially that he believes that Commerce Secretary Wilbur Ross, who oversees the census - that he made the wrong call by adding a citizenship question before deciding to use existing government records as much as possible and that he made a, quote, ""smorgasbord of violations"" of administrative procedure act, including cherry-picking evidence to support his decision to add a citizenship question.
Quickly before I let you go, Hansi, you said the judge expects his decision to be appealed.
I mean, the timing here is tricky, right?
We're a year away from the start of the 2020 census.
Is that enough time for this to play out however it's going to ultimately play out in the courts?
It's going to be very, very tight.
And we can't forget that there are lawsuits also in California and Maryland.
There are - all underway.
And we're going to keep on watching to see where this all - how this all unfolds.
NPR's Hansi Lo Wang covering all things census-related - thank you, Hansi.
You're welcome.
The president will be in the Middle East until next week.
Iran is not on the president's itinerary, but as he travels to some of the Persian Gulf states, Iran will be on the agenda, and pretty high up on that agenda.
Mr.
Bush says the international community must isolate and contain Iran.
But after a recent intelligence report revealed that Iran is not building nuclear weapons, the U.S.
is losing international support for that policy.
Marc Lynch is a political science professor at George Washington University and joins us now.
And Mr. Lynch, welcome to the program.
Let's take Egypt and Saudi Arabia, two U.S.
allies the president will visit.
What is their position on Iran, and how does it differ from President Bush's?
Well, I think both Saudi Arabia and Egypt have been very worried about Iran for quite some time.
And dating back about a year, they were actually at the forefront of Arab efforts to try and contain Iran and to kind of mobilize support for the confrontation against Iran.
The Orville Peck character matches the haunting, sometimes eerie sounds of his debut album, ""Pony.
Just like an early human embryo would do in the womb.
Now, these are nowhere near being fully formed embryos, but the scientists are trying to get as close as they can.
Essentially, we're trying to, in a way, recreate a human embryo in a dish, starting from stem cells.
Why?

To study them and hopefully make big discoveries about basic human biology and development - discoveries that could lead to new ways to prevent miscarriages, treat infertility, avoid birth defects and, Brivanlou says, maybe even unlock the mysteries of aging.
What is the clock in the human embryo that says, OK, now you have to do this, now you have to do this?
There's a tempo.
It's like music.
And understanding of the molecular basis of this tempo would also open up the doors to understanding aging.
I start to wonder, though - how far could these embryoids go?
Could they become an embryo?
Well, probably not, but they could become something that looks very much like an embryo.
But how much like an embryo could they become?
That's a very good question, and we'll see.
At the moment, we don't know.
This is something that's very hard right now for us to understand.
Because these embryoids could let them sidestep something called the 14-day rule.
It prohibits scientists from keeping human embryos alive in the lab for more than 14 days.
Two weeks is when human embryos usually start to show something called the primitive streak.
It's the beginning of the central nervous system.
It's also when embryos can't split into twins anymore, so some say that's when they become unique individuals.
But Simunovic says these embryoids aren't subject to the 14-day rule.
We are not technically or ethically limited to studying these cells and studying these structures.
You're not.
We're not.
But there's a big debate about that.
In some other embryoids, Brivanlou says the scientists have been surprised to see the early signs of the primitive streak.
We started - I think we just started seeing the beginning of the early primitive streak.
So doesn't that raise questions about the 14-day rule potentially?
Sure.
I mean, it should.
If people want to discuss this as part of the 14 rule, why not?
And they are.
Insoo Hyung is a bioethicist at Case Western Reserve University.
These are cells that are behaving, interacting with one another and doing what embryos do in the womb.
It's almost like this an inner budding life in this model that you created in the dish.
That's what's was remarkable.
Are we recreating a life that in the right circumstances, if you were to transfer this to the womb, it would continue its journey?
So at what point is your model of the embryo basically an embryo?
And Hyung is not alone in his concerns.
George Daley is the dean of the Harvard Medical School and a leading stem cell researcher.
He says scientists have been preparing for the day when stem cell research might raise these kinds of difficult questions.

You know, I think what prospects people are concerned about are the kinds of dystopian worlds that were written about by Aldous Huxley in ""Brave New World,"" you know, where human reproduction is done on a highly mechanized scale in a petri dish.
Now, Daley stresses scientists are nowhere near making that sort of thing possible and may never get there.
But science moves quickly these days, so Daley says it's important to make sure all these experiments undergo intense ethical review.
Brivanlou doubts his embryoids would ever actually become real embryos, but, he says, all of his work goes through many layers of careful vetting.
And he's moving cautiously in the hopes of answering some big questions.
I think science and philosophy are trying, to a large extent, to answer the most basic questions, which are - Where are we from?
Where are we going?
So if I can provide a glimpse of where we come from, what happened to us for us to get here - I think that, to me, is a strong enough rationale to continue pushing this.
Rob Stein, NPR News, New York.
The death penalty hit a milestone this week.
The 1,000th person was executed since capital punishment was restored in 1976.
We asked NPR's Laura Sullivan to go back 15 years, to one execution, to see how it had affected the lives of the people closest to it.
Now a caution.
Some of the material in this report may be inappropriate for younger listeners.
The crime was one of the worst in Maryland's history.
A man in his 40s goes on a four-day killing spree, murdering three teen-agers for apparently no reason.
Two of his victims were Melody Pistorio and Billy Winebrenner.
They were high school sweethearts.
The saddest thing, when I came home after finding out about her murder was her schoolbooks were there.
Donna Dillon is Melody's mother.
Melody was 14, Billy, 16, when they were shot in the head at a gas station in Baltimore on the last day of summer.
She had all of her papers and her schoolbooks and everything written--you know, ready to go to school.
And she had `I love Billy' on her notebooks.
Just a few hours' drive from the gas station, out on the Maryland shore, another teen-ager, 18-year-old Greg Taylor, was also dead, left in the woods by a logging road.
His parents had been searching for him for almost five days.
On the fifth day, the Maryland police called Lois Burton(ph) and her husband to police headquarters.
They sat us in this little teeny, tiny room and they told us that they had some good news and they had some bad news, that Greg was found but that he was dead.
That's how they said it.
Greg Taylor had been shot in the head three times after he picked up a hitchhiker.
His hitchhiker, and murderer, was John Thanos.
There's never been any doubt that John Thanos killed Melody Pistorio, Billy Winebrenner and Greg Taylor, and he wasn't sorry about it, either.
At one point in his trial, he told the judge he would like to have the teens exhumed so he could shoot them again.
Melody's mother, Donna Dillon.
But now they both seemed to be backing away a bit.
I don't think that's because they've become less worried.

I think it's more to do with losing a bit of patience, perhaps, a little bit of confidence in the United States, and also just beginning to prepare themselves to accommodate to the reality of rising Iranian influence.
So why is it better for them to be more accommodating to rising Iranian influence rather than confrontational?
Well, especially in the Gulf, the price of oil, as you know, is sky high, and that's good for business in the Gulf.
And the prospect of a war, I think, really frightens a lot of people in the Gulf.
And at the same time, you have to understand that people in the Gulf have been living with Iran for quite some time.
This isn't a new issue for them.
They see Iran as a challenge, but they see it as something, I think, which can be managed.
The president talks a lot about containing Iran.
And I'm wondering, what will the heads of state say to him about that policy?
Will they try to gently shift him in another direction?
I think that one of the keys to any containment policy, whether it's Iran or anybody else, is you really need to have the enthusiastic support of neighboring states.
It's the only way really to make sanctions work or to make any kind of containment policy work.
And I think what you're seeing now is a lot of the most important Arab states, especially in the Gulf, who have really lost that enthusiasm.

But aren't they worried that with a rising Shiite power in Iran and perhaps also in Iraq, that - that that might spur restive minority Shia populations in their own countries and create some discord there?
They've certainly been quite worried about that.
And frankly, you don't even need to have a Shia minority to be worried about that.
If you take Egypt, for example, which has no Shia to speak off, you had as much anti-Shia hysteria gripping Cairo last winter as you did in the Gulf.

And there, for example, in Cairo, a big part of the problem was that when you had the war between Israel and Hezbollah, you saw a fair number of quite Sunni Egyptians who were sympathizing very vocally with Hezbollah against Israel.
